title: Chapter 35: The Betrayal
content: The Signal Key lay on the common room table like a small, pulsing heart torn from something mechanical and alive. The whole community was gathered — everyone who'd made it back after the raid, everyone who could still be counted. Forty-three invisibles in a room built for thirty, breathing recycled air, watching the device with a mixture of hope and terror.

Wren stood at the table's head. The files were spread beside the Key — Subject 01 through Subject 13, the terminated, the contained, the missing, and the two who'd broken out. The evidence. Vance's operation exposed in ink and data.

Ash sat against the far wall, his leg bound with salvaged bandages, his face grey with pain and blood loss. He hadn't let anyone treat him properly. He was watching the room. Watching the exits. Watching Lira.

Lira stood near the rear of the crowd, arms folded, expression composed as always. She'd been quiet since they returned. No celebration. No relief. Just that watchful stillness that Wren had mistaken for composure.

"We should examine the Key first," Silas said. He was standing close to the device, studying it with the wary fascination of a man approaching a loaded weapon. "Understand what it does before we activate it."
"The schematics are in the files," Wren said. "Vance designed it to reverse the ANX-00 signal disruption. One activation should restore our neural patterns to their pre-treatment state."
"Should."
"Should is all we've got."

The room murmured. Arguments started — the same arguments as before, but sharper now, edged with the adrenaline of the raid and the exhaustion of the night. Some wanted to activate immediately. Some wanted to wait. Some wanted to destroy it, fearing what "restore" might actually mean.

Wren let them argue. She was looking at the Key. Its pulse had changed since she'd taken it — slower, deeper, as if it had settled into something. Into her. The resonance was still there, the frequency that had matched the moment she touched it.
She was about to speak when she noticed.
Lira was gone.

Not moved. Not stepped outside. Gone — from a room with one entrance, a room full of people, a room where vanishing should have been impossible. The space where Lira had stood was empty, and no one had noticed because no one was looking, and Wren felt the floor drop out from under her.
"Where's Lira?" Her voice cut the arguments like a blade.
Silence. The room turned. Heads swiveled. The space near the back wall was bare.
"When did anyone last see her?" Wren demanded.
No one could say. She'd been there. Then she wasn't. Like smoke. Like a lie.
Wren's hands went to the table — to the main data terminal, the one Lira had used to project the floor plans. It was still running. She pulled up the access log.

Lira's drive had been connected for six minutes. In those six minutes, it had done more than display schematics. It had copied. Uploaded. Every file on the communal server — the safe house locations, the supply route maps, the emergency protocols, the names and last-known positions of every invisible in the Underlayer network.
All of it transmitted. Eighteen seconds ago. To an external server registered to NeuroScale Pharmaceuticals.
The blood drained from Wren's face.
"She's a plant," Wren said. Her voice didn't sound like her own. "Lira. She was never ex-NeuroScale. She's their asset. She's been theirs the whole time."
The room erupted.

Fear first — cold, animal, paralyzing. Then panic. Darby started to cry. Mouse pressed himself against the wall. Two people moved for the exit. Three more grabbed bags, supplies, anything within reach.
Silas's voice cut through: "How long do we have?"

Wren checked the transmission data. The upload had completed. The response ping was automated — received, processed, deploying. Standard NeuroScale containment protocol. From upload to deployment: forty-five minutes, maximum.
"Less than an hour," she said.

The fear became a living thing. It moved through the room like contagion, touching everyone. The Hounds were coming — not scouts this time, not a single unit testing the tunnels. A full pack. Six units. Thermal imaging, sonar, containment protocols. And now they knew exactly where to look.

"We fight." Ash was standing. He shouldn't have been able to stand — the leg wound, the blood loss — but he was on his feet, his voice cutting through the noise. "We know these tunnels better than they do. We set traps, we—"
"We die," Wren said. The words tasted like metal. "Six Hounds, containment team support, and they have our exact coordinates. We fight, we lose. Not maybe. Definitely."
"Then what?" Ash's voice cracked. "We run? Again? To where? They'll find us wherever—"
"We scatter. Not deeper. Out. Into the city. Into the population. Six Hounds can't track forty-three people through eight million."
"And the Underlayer?"

Wren didn't answer. She didn't have to. They all knew. The Underlayer was done. The safe houses, the supply caches, the routes and passages and hiding places — all of it was compromised. And with it, the community that had formed around them. The Glitch Garden with its mural. The common room with its salvaged furniture. The place where invisible people had become visible to each other.
Gone. All of it.

"Evacuate," Wren said. "Now. Everyone takes what they can carry, uses the emergency routes, and gets above ground. Don't go home — they'll watch your homes. Go to shelters, hospitals, train stations. Places with crowds. Places where heat signatures blend."
"You're asking us to abandon everything," Harlow said. The old man's voice was hollow.
"I'm asking you to survive."

The room fractured. Some moved immediately — practical, decisive, grabbing bags and bolting for the exits. Others lingered, paralyzed, unable to leave the only home they'd had. A woman named Kade stood in front of the Glitch Garden mural, one hand raised to the painted face of a screaming figure, her palm flat against the wall like she could absorb it through her skin.
Wren went to her. "Kade. We have to go."
"The mural," Kade whispered. "It's the only proof we—"
"I know."
"It's the only—"
"Kade." Wren's hand found her shoulder. "We're the proof. Not the wall. Us. Living. Breathing. Walking out of here."
Kade's hand dropped. She picked up her bag. She left.

Wren moved through the space, herding, pushing, pulling. The evacuation was chaotic — no formation, no order, just terror propelling bodies toward exits. Mouse went with Darby. Harlow took the north tunnel with three others. Silas went last, his face a mask of grief, his cane clicking on the concrete with a rhythm that sounded like a closing door.
Ash was still in the common room.
"I'm not leaving the Key," he said.
"I'm not asking you to."
"Someone needs to stay and slow them down. Buy time for the others."
"No."
"Wren—"
"No." She was in front of him now, her hand on his chest, feeling his heartbeat — rapid, pained, alive. "You're not a martyr. You're not a sacrifice. You're coming with me."
"The Hounds—"
"Will track our heat signatures regardless. Whether we're here or above ground doesn't change that. What changes is that above ground, there are eight million other signatures to hide in."
His jaw tightened. The blood on his face had dried to a dark crust. He looked wrecked. He looked real.
"Move," she said.
He moved.

They took the east tunnel — the one Lira had shown them, the one that led to a drainage outflow near the river. The irony was acid: the route their betrayer had provided was still the fastest way out. Wren didn't have time to savor it.

Behind them, the Underlayer emptied. Footsteps echoed in the corridors, grew sparse, faded. The common room stood open and hollow. The mural watched it all with painted screams. The Signal Key pulsed in Wren's jacket, and the files were a hard weight in her bag, and somewhere above them, the city hummed with eight million lives that didn't know what was coming.
At the drainage outflow, Wren stopped. Turned. Looked back.
The tunnel stretched dark and empty behind them. No sound. No movement. Just the faint vibration of machinery — the Hounds, descending, closing on a home that was no longer a home.
Behind her, the city. The rain. The crowds.
Ahead of her, nothing but the next breath.
She took it.
Then she ran.
